text: Runners who recently made one or more changes in velocity, distance, or frequency had a significantly higher injury risk compared with non-injured runners (p = 0.037).
Increasing average weekly running distance by more than 30% is associated with higher injury risk compared to increases less than 10% (HR = 1.59, 95% CI: 0.96-2.66).
Runners increased their total running distance by an average of 86% more in the week before injury onset compared with other weeks (95% CI: 12-159%, p = 0.026).
specific_value: 86% mean difference in weekly distance before injury

text: No difference in injury risk was found between a 10% and a 24% average increase in weekly volume (HR = 0.8, 95% CI: 0.6-1.3), suggesting moderate weekly increases up to ~24% may not elevate risk.
The overall evidence supporting an association between sudden training load changes and running-related injury is very limited, with only four eligible studies identified in a systematic review.
Both sudden increases and decreases in training load have been suggested to play a role in running-related injury development, not just increases alone.
